summary: Hypocalcemia commonly follows thyroidectomy due to accidental injury or removal of parathyroid tissue. Its classic manifestations include paresthesias, muscle cramps, and tetany—precisely matching the reported symptoms here. Key ECG abnormalities linked directly to hypocalcemia involve delayed repolarization causing QT interval extension rather than peaked T-waves seen more often during acute metabolic acidosis or magnesium deficiency. Thus, while both options B and C could theoretically be considered depending upon context, literature specifically links hypocalcemia primarily with increased duration of ventricular depolarization reflected via extended QT periods instead of peak T wave formation observed typically under different conditions such as severe hyponatremia or potassium imbalance scenarios where altered membrane potentials affect early phases of action potential generation differently across myocardial cells types involved within conduction system dynamics compared to skeletal muscles affected by decreased Its classic manifestations include paresthesias, muscle cramps, and tetany—precisely matching the reported symptoms here. Key ECG abnormalities linked directly to hypocalcemia involve delayed repolarization causing QT interval extension rather than peaked T-waves seen more often during acute metabolic acidosis or magnesium deficiency. Thus, while both options B and C could theoretically be considered depending upon context, literature specifically links hypocalcemia primarily with increased duration of ventricular depolarization
